name: litellm-acompletion
input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools. Task: Analyse the data from MCP tools and provide insights."}, {"role": "assistant", "content": null, "tool_calls": [{"id": "call_yJVT75Phu6BRTkVAlGz86G04", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_list_in_namespace", "arguments": "{"namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_PEh5WvQoc9DRDSXGuIFN8eek", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "events_list", "arguments": "{"namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_W5ZxOHNt5o6PlZJY8PtB3CTU", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_top", "arguments": "{"all_namespaces": false, "namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_kM1d0rjZ21ztSYBPJreThFDH", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "list_metrics", "arguments": "{"limit": 50, "filter_pattern": "chaos|fault|inject|disrupt", "refresh_cache": false}"}}]}, {"role": "tool", "tool_call_id": "call_yJVT75Phu6BRTkVAlGz86G04", "content": "NAMESPACE   APIVERSION   KIND   NAME                                     READY   STATUS    RESTARTS        AGE   IP             NODE                                NOMINATED NODE   READINESS GATES   LABELS

fault_reasoning.pod-memory-hog: Deterministic temporal assignment: this event's startTime falls inside this fault's [injection_timestamp, injection_end_timestamp] window and no other fault was in flight.

name: litellm-acompletion
input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],

    }

  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],

  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,

    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },

    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",

      "category": "category",

      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools. fault_reasoning.pod-memory-hog: Deterministic temporal assignment: this event's startTime falls inside this fault's [injection_timestamp, injection_end_timestamp] window and no other fault was in flight.

name: litellm-acompletion
input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },

name: litellm-acompletion
input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools. Task: Analyse the data from MCP tools and provide insights."}], "tools": [{"type": "function", "function": {"name": "configuration_view", "description": "Get the current Kubernetes configuration content as a kubeconfig YAML", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"minified": {"type": "boolean", "description": "Return a minified version of the configuration. If set to true, keeps only the current-context and the relevant pieces of the configuration for that context. If set to false, all contexts, clusters, auth-infos, and users are returned in the configuration. (Optional, default true)"}}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "events_list", "description": "List Kubernetes events (warnings, errors, state changes) for debugging and troubleshooting in the current cluster from all namespaces", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Optional Namespace to retrieve the events from. If not provided, will list events from all namespaces"}}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "namespaces_list", "description": "List all the Kubernetes namespaces in the current cluster", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "nodes_log", "description": "Get logs from a Kubernetes node (kubelet, kube-proxy, or other system logs). fault_reasoning.pod-memory-hog: Deterministic temporal assignment: this event's startTime falls inside this fault's [injection_timestamp, injection_end_timestamp] window and no other fault was in flight.

name: litellm-acompletion
input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools. Task: Analyse the data from MCP tools and provide insights."}, {"role": "assistant", "content": null, "tool_calls": [{"id": "call_decudp4HzDuN5M8gyoev48px", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_list_in_namespace", "arguments": "{"namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_nhryT96xf7Ik1vJNiyBJOxME", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "events_list", "arguments": "{"namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_a2Zxc6VLyZeNhLXi6Sv06BQn", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_top", "arguments": "{"all_namespaces": false, "namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_GbZ0CV8cDhE5iZ6NopW640ii", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "list_metrics", "arguments": "{"limit": 50, "offset": 0, "filter_pattern": "chaos|fault|inject|disrupt", "refresh_cache": false}"}}]}, {"role": "tool", "tool_call_id": "call_decudp4HzDuN5M8gyoev48px", "content": "NAMESPACE   APIVERSION   KIND   NAME                                     READY   STATUS    RESTARTS        AGE   IP             NODE                                NOMINATED NODE   READINESS GATES   LABELS
sock-shop   v1           Pod    carts-6b5d88f967-l7m7c                   1/1     Running   0               28m   10.244.1.83    aks-agentpool-17671638-vmss000003   <none>           <none>            app=sock-shop,name=carts,pod-template-hash=6b5d88f967
sock-shop   v1           Pod    carts-db-794bdddcd7-2q5zh                1/1     Running   0               28m   10.244.1.250   aks-agentpool-17671638-vmss000003   <none>           <none>            app=sock-shop,name=carts-db,pod-template-hash=794bdddcd7

name: litellm-acompletion
input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools. Task: Analyse the data from MCP tools and provide insights."}], "tools": [{"type": "function", "function": {"name": "configuration_view", "description": "Get the current Kubernetes configuration content as a kubeconfig YAML", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"minified": {"type": "boolean", "description": "Return a minified version of the configuration. input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools. Task: Analyse the data from MCP tools and provide insights."}, {"role": "assistant", "content": null, "tool_calls": [{"id": "call_bmfvz3TWb2wrCfkb69VMwQmQ", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_list_in_namespace", "arguments": "{"namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_tmPShxjK5AHFHEggPUUIFGMW", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "events_list", "arguments": "{"namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_ZibvR9Y7cO4dxF0eoOgupeQB", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_top", "arguments": "{"all_namespaces": false, "namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_19EPiGx6vtjfxNT8a1igojNl", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "list_metrics", "arguments": "{"limit": 1000, "filter_pattern": "chaos|fault|inject|disrupt", "refresh_cache": false}"}}]}, {"role": "tool", "tool_call_id": "call_bmfvz3TWb2wrCfkb69VMwQmQ", "content": "NAMESPACE   APIVERSION   KIND   NAME                                     READY   STATUS    RESTARTS      AGE   IP             NODE                                NOMINATED NODE   READINESS GATES   LABELS
